coredata.dc:title: The effect of adjacent double-strand DNA on the G-triplex-ThT complex fluorescence intensity enhancement and its application in TNOS and Hg2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chengke
Li, Jiangyu
Wang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we have found that adjacent double-strand DNA (dsDNA) can enhance the fluorescence intensity of the G-triplex (G31)-thioflavin T (ThT) complex. By connecting the newly formed dsDNA with the G31 sequence, terminator nopaline synthase (TNOS) gene and Hg2+ were detected. The intermolecular duplex hybridization (e.g., TNOS gene and its complementary DNA), or the intramolecular mismatched thymine (T)-Hg2+-T pairs induced the formation of dsDNA with planar morphology, which resulted in the increased binding capacity of adjacent G31 to ThT, as well as the enhanced fluorescence intensity of G31-ThT complex. A versatile ‘turn-on’ fluorescence scaffold was developed for discriminating transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans and detecting Hg2+ in lake water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
G-triplex
Hg2+
TNOS
ThT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dsDNA linked G31 complex
Analysis of TNOS using the fluorescent method
Analysis of Hg2+ using the fluorescent method
The detection of TNOS in soybean samples
The principle and feasibility of the fluorescent method
The optimization of the experimental conditions
The detection of TNOS and Hg2+ in aqueous solutions
The utility of this fluorescent method
The CD spectroscopy and PAGE experiment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21305032 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2014M551522 ), and Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds ( 1402073B ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence emission spectra (A), and the fluorescence intensity at 495 nm (B) of solutions in the presence of ThT, TNOS + ThT, cDNA-G31+ThT, cDNA-G31+TNOS, and cDNA-G31+TNOS + ThT, respectively. The concentrations of TNOS, cDNA-G31 and ThT are 150 nM, 200 nM, and 6 μM, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra (C) and the fluorescence intensity at 495 nm (D) of solutions in the presence of ThT, Hg2++ThT, 6T-G31+ThT, 6T-G31+ Hg2+ and 6T-G31+ThT + Hg2+, respectively. The concentrations of 6T-G31, ThT, and Hg2+ are 150 nM, 4 μM, and 300 nM, respectively. The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.

The fluorescence spectra of the solutions in the presence of different concentrations of TNOS (A) and Hg2+ (C); the changes of F/F0 as a function of the concentration of the TNOS (B) and Hg2+ (D), respectively. The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.

The fluorescence spectra (A) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity at 495 nm (B) of the solutions in the presence of 150 nM TNOS or other species. The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.

The fluorescence intensity at 495 nm of the solutions in the absence or presence of different samples, the Cnon-GMO1 to CGMO3 (from left to right) corresponding to 25%, 50%, and 100% of the original concentration of extracted solutions from non-transgenic soybean and transgenic soybean, respectively, the error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results (A). The electrophoresis results of PCR products acquired from transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans, lane 1 to lane 4 (from left to right) indicate DNA marker (1), non-transgenic soybean (2), transgenic soybean sample (3), and buffer alone (4), respectively (B). The CD spectra of solutions in the presence of ThT, TNOS, cDNA-G31, TNOS + ThT, cDNA-G31+ThT, cDNA-G31+TNOS, or cDNA-G31+TNOS + ThT, respectively (C). The PAGE results: lanes 1–7 indicate DNA marker (1), cDNA-G31 (2), TNOS (3), cDNA-G31+ThT (4), TNOS + ThT (5), cDNA-G31+TNOS (6), and cDNA-G31+TNOS + ThT (7), respectively (D).
Schematic illustration of the label-free fluorescence probes for TNOS and Hg2+ detection based on the formation of dsDNA-linked G-triplex-ThT complexes. The image is not drawn to scale.

head.ce:title: The effect of adjacent double-strand DNA on the G-triplex-ThT complex fluorescence intensity enhancement and its application in TNOS and Hg2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Bionanotechnology, Ludong University, Yantai, 264025, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Bionanotechnology, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai, 264025, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Bionanotechnology, Ludong University, Yantai, 264025, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we have found that adjacent double-strand DNA (dsDNA) can enhance the fluorescence intensity of the G-triplex (G31)-thioflavin T (ThT) complex. By connecting the newly formed dsDNA with the G31 sequence, terminator nopaline synthase (TNOS) gene and Hg2+ were detected. The intermolecular duplex hybridization (e.g., TNOS gene and its complementary DNA), or the intramolecular mismatched thymine (T)-Hg2+-T pairs induced the formation of dsDNA with planar morphology, which resulted in the increased binding capacity of adjacent G31 to ThT, as well as the enhanced fluorescence intensity of G31-ThT complex. A versatile ‘turn-on’ fluorescence scaffold was developed for discriminating transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans and detecting Hg2+ in lake water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety problems are getting more and more attention in recent years [1], like mycotoxins (e.g., aflatoxin A, ochratoxin A [2]), pesticides (e.g., acetamiprid [3]) in cereals and vegetables, antibiotics in animal-derived food (e.g., kanamycin, tetracycline in chicken meat and eggs, milk, pork and beef [4]). Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) are also getting people's attention. More than 50 countries and regions have strengthened the management of and issued GMO labeling regulations [5–7]. Transgenic soybeans are frequently consumed in the world, and the terminator nopaline synthase (TNOS) gene sequence from Agrobacterium tumefaciens is widely used in GMO development and can be found in 42.3% of current commercialized GM crops, while it is nonexistent in the genomes of ordinary plants [8,9]. Therefore, the TNOS gene is often targeted for screening GM content, and positive results from initial screening are further analyzed to reveal the identity and quantity of specific GM events [5,10]. On the other hand, heavy metal ions in the lake, river, and underground water are harmful to human health [11–13]. For example, Hg2+ exhibits a severe threat to the environment and human health due to its high toxicity and easy accumulation in the human body [14–16]. It is important to develop a simple, sensitive, and versatile method to detect GM products and Hg2+ in food and environmental samples.

The traditional methods for food safety detection including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and chromatography for mycotoxins and antibiotics detection (e.g., aflatoxin A and kanamycin); inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) for heavy metal ions detection [17,18]. These methods always need sophisticated instruments or specific antibodies and skilled operators, which limits their application for quickly and conveniently detecting different harmful species. The fluorescent method based on the interaction between DNA is getting lots of attention, due to its advantages, like high sensitivity and selectivity, and easy-to-conduct by changing the sequence of DNA. For example, the G-quadruplex DNA contained multiple guanines (G) that can bind hemin and crystal violet molecules [19], and enhance the fluorescence of complex. Therefore, ATP, K+, kanamycin, and ochratoxin A were successfully detected by this mechanism [19–21]. Recently, the G-triplex DNA has also been extensively studied due to its flexible structure, easy to design, and convenience to conjugate with other functional groups (e.g., fluorescence molecules) without compromising the specific conformation integrity [22].

Since there are only three G-rich strands that can cooperatively bind with Thioflavin T (ThT), it is valuable to find a simple method to enhance the stability and fluorescence intensity of G-triplex-ThT, as well as to improve the sensitivity of the detection. The conformation rigidity of the duplex DNA complex is increased compared with its single-strand DNA counterpart. Therefore, in this experiment, intermolecular double-strand DNA (dsDNA) structure was formed through hybridization between the TNOS segment and its cDNA, and intramolecular dsDNA structure was formed through the mismatched T-Hg2+-T pairs originated from the strong affinity between thymine (T) bases and Hg2+. We found that the fluorescence intensity of the G-triplex-ThT complex enhanced significantly after dsDNA adjacent to the G-triplex structure. Based on this principle, TNOS and Hg2+ were sensitively detected in transgenic soybeans and lake water, respectively. The principle of fluorescent signal enhancement was also studied using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy and electrophoresis experiments.

Tris (hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris), 4S Green™ nucleic acid stain, DNA, and EZ-10 spin column plant genomic DNA extraction kit were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences of DNA used in this work are listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information). ThT was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (USA). Hg(NO3)2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, CuCl2, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl2, AgNO3, MgCl2 and NaCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The TNOS contained transgenic soybean imported from Argentina and was provided by Sinograin Zhenjiang Grains & Oils Quality Testing Center (Zhenjiang, China). The real lake water was obtained from Mirror Lake on the campus of Jiangsu University. All other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled water (dd H2O) was used throughout the experiment.

The fluorescent spectra were recorded on a Tecan Infinite 200 PRO microplate reader (Mannedorf, Swiss) or F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 435 nm and emission wavelengths from 465 nm to 545 nm. The CD spectra were recorded with a JASCO J-815 circular dichroism spectrometer (JASCO Co., Ltd., Japan). The pH value of the working buffer was adjusted using a Leici PHS-3E pH meter (INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The 4% (w/w) DNA agarose and 20% (w/w) polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) were conducted using a DYY-6C electrophoresis system (Nanjing, China). The DNA bands were recorded with a Haiqing digital camera system (Nanjing, China). The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) experiment was conducted on a QX100 droplet digital PCR system (Bio-Rad Laboratories, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dsDNA linked G31 complex
ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL ssDNA-G31 (i.e., 11A-G31, 14A-G31, 17A-G31 or 20A-G31) and 20 μL cDNA (i.e., 11T, 14T, 17T or 20T) were mixed in the 140 μL Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 7.3, 40 mM Na+ and 40 mM Mg2+) in the presence of 20 μL ThT at 15 °C, respectively, the final concentrations of DNA and ThT are 200 nM and 6 μM. The fluorescence spectra of the dsDNA-G31-ThT mixtures were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TNOS using the fluorescent method
ce:section.ce:para: cDNA-G31 (G31 linked with complementary DNA (cDNA) of TNOS, 10 μL, 200 nM) was mixed with 10 μL different concentrations of TNOS, then 10 μL ThT, 20 μL NaCl, 20 μL MgCl2, and 130 μL Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 7.3) were added and reacted for 60 min at room temperature; finally, fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ using the fluorescent method
ce:section.ce:para: 6T-G31 (G31 linked with DNA sequence containing 6 thymine bases, 20 μL) was mixed with 20 μL different concentrations of Hg(NO3)2, then 10 μL ThT, 150 μL phosphate-buffered solution (PB, 100 mM, pH 7.5) were added and reacted for 10 min at room temperature; finally, the fluorescence spectra of mixtures were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TNOS in soybean samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three batches of soybeans (at least 50 g of raw soybeans per batch) were carefully crushed to pass through a 100-mesh sieve using a stainless crusher (CHIGO technology Co. Ltd., Foshan, China). DNA in the samples (n = 3) was extracted from these three batches of soybean powder (100 mg per batch) using the commercial EZ-10 spin column plant genomic DNA extraction kit (Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) following the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, 100 mg soybean powder was mixed with 1 mL extraction buffer (2 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.5) for 10 min, the supernatant of the mixture was carefully transferred to the spin column, and waited for 10 min to enable all the DNA adsorbed onto the column. After the spin column is centrifuged (Eppendorf Centrifuge 5425r, Eppendorf Co. Ltd., Hamburg, Germany) at 1000 rounds per minute (rpm) for 5 min to remove the soluble species (like proteins and carbohydrates), 1 mL washing buffer was slowly added to the column to desorb the DNA for 10 min, the spin column was centrifuged again at 1000 rpm for 5 min, the DNA contained solutions were diluted for 2–4 times using Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.3), 25%, 50%, and 100% concentrations of the original solution were obtained. These solutions were amplified by the PCR method and analyzed by 4% (w/w) agarose gel electrophoresis. The PCR products were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and frozen in an ice bath to obtain single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) solution. Finally, the ssDNA was detected using our proposed fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle and feasibility of the fluorescent method
ce:section.ce:para: To validate our assumption that adjacent dsDNA can enhance the fluorescence of the G31-ThT complex, as well as to confirm the enhancement of fluorescence intensity is related to the length of dsDNA segments, a series of ssDNA with different lengths (contain 11, 14, 17 and 20 adenine) was selected and linked to G31, then dsDNA were formed in the presence of cDNA (contain 11, 14, 17 and 20 thymine). As shown in Fig. S1, when the length of dsDNA increased from 11 to 20, the fluorescence intensities of the dsDNA-G31-ThT complexes increased continually. More base pairs for longer dsDNA resulted in the enhanced stability of dsDNA, therefore the general principle of this assay may be concluded: 1) adjacent dsDNA can enhance the fluorescence intensity of the G31-ThT complex, 2) the fluorescence intensity is enhanced much more significant when the dsDNA length and stability are increased.

By using this principle, we have proposed a new “turn-on” fluorescent method for TNOS and Hg2+ detection. As shown in Scheme 1 , the cDNA of TNOS or ssDNA contains multiple thymine (T) is connected to the G31 segment (5′-TGGGTAGGGCGGG-3′, G31), G31 is obtained by truncating five bases in the 3′ end of G-quadruplex DNA, named CatG4 [22]. The G31 sequence can form a G-triplex structure and bind with ThT molecule, therefore inducing an enhanced fluorescence signal compared with ThT alone. After TNOS interacts with cDNA-G31, or Hg2+ interacts with 6T-G31, due to duplex hybridization between TNOS and cDNA, or the formation of intramolecular T-Hg2+-T pairs, dsDNA adjacent to the G-triplex-ThT complex is formed and significantly the fluorescence intensity of the mixture is enhanced. This phenomenon is due to the enhanced stability of the G-triplex-ThT complex, as well as the planar morphology of the final complex, which favors the binding of ThT, and restricts the rotation of aromatic rings in ThT, thereby results in the improved fluorescence intensity of ThT.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the assay, cDNA-G31 and/or TNOS were mixed with ThT, and the fluorescent spectra were acquired. As shown in Fig. 1 A–B, ThT had a relatively low fluorescence signal no matter whether there was TNOS or not; the mixture of cDNA-G31 and TNOS also showed negligible fluorescence. In contrast, after adding cDNA-G31 into ThT solution, the fluorescence signal of the mixture was increased, which was consistent with the previous report that G31 can bind with ThT and forms G-triplex-ThT complex, thereby induces enhanced fluorescence intensity of ThT [22]. In addition, it is interesting that after adding target DNA (i.e., TNOS) into the cDNA-G31 and ThT solution, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was further enhanced six times. This result indicated that the duplex structure formed by the hybridization of TNOS and cDNA facilitated the interaction of the G31 segment with ThT, therefore leading to enhanced fluorescence intensity.

To further verify the fluorescence signal enhancement phenomenon is because the adjacent duplex DNA induced improved stability of the G31-ThT complex, intramolecular dsDNA structure was formed through the formation of T-Hg2+-T pairs. As shown in Fig. 1C–D, the fluorescence signal was kept at a low level when Hg2+ interacted with ThT or 6T-G31. The fluorescence intensity increased after ThT interacted with 6T-G31 and further enhanced in the presence of Hg2+, due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T intramolecular double-strand structure [23]. These experimental results demonstrate the versatility of this method, i.e., the intermolecular or intramolecular dsDNA adjacent to the G-triplex structure can enhance the fluorescence intensity of the G-triplex-ThT complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of the experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: It is essential to determine the optimal conditions to detect TNOS and Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescent intensity of ThT increased with increasing the concentrations of cDNA-G31 no matter whether there was TNOS or not; however, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced in the presence of TNOS. Here, F/F0 was used to study the changes of fluorescence signals; F and F0 referred to the fluorescence intensity of solutions at 495 nm (F495nm) in the presence or absence of the target (e.g., TNOS), respectively. As shown in Fig. S3A, the F/F0 of the solutions gradually increased with increasing cDNA-G31 concentration from 50 to 200 nM and maintained at a constant value when the cDNA-G31 was more than 200 nM. This is because more cDNA-G31 can generate more cDNA-G31-ThT complexes, thus resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. When the concentration of cDNA-G31 was higher than 200 nM, all the TNOS was used for the hybridization reaction, and the relative fluorescence intensity of solutions (i.e., F/F0) in the absence and presence of TNOS was not changed.

Since ThT was used as the fluorescent indicator in this system, the fluorescence intensity changes of solutions in the presence of ThT were studied. As shown in Fig. S2B, the fluorescence intensity increased when the ThT concentration changed from 0 to 8 μM, and decreased if more than 8 μM ThT was used in the presence of TNOS, but the signal was still increased in the absence of TNOS. As shown in Fig. S3B, the F/F0 of the solutions reached a maximum when 6 μM ThT was used. This phenomenon was because the more ThT molecules, the more cDNA-G31-ThT complexes with planar conformation were formed. Due to the aromatic ring's free rotation of ThT was inhibited, the fluorescence intensity of the complex was enhanced. In our recent work, CD spectroscopy and electrophoresis results indicated that the insertion of ThT into the G-triplex played an adverse role in the integrity of the G-triplex, especially for ThT at a high concentration. The destruction or misfolding of the G-triplex structure resulted in the fluorescence of the complex decreased [24]. As shown in Fig. S4, increased concentrations of ThT interacted with a constant amount of G31, and the fluorescence signal of G31-ThT mixture was enhanced if ThT was at a low concentration, but decreased if a higher concentration of ThT was used. This phenomenon was consistent with the previous report [25].

The DNA hybridization process was affected by Na+ and Mg2+ ions. Therefore, their effects on TNOS detection were evaluated, as shown in Figs. S2C and S3C, the fluorescence signal intensities of the solutions in the presence of TNOS were gradually increased from 0 to 40 mM Na+. The F/F0 reached a plateau if more than 40 mM Na+ was added. This was because Na + ions can screen the repulsive interaction between two negatively charged DNA strands, and accelerate the formation of duplex DNA [26]. When duplex structure are completely formed, further increasing the Na+ ions was not able to enhance the fluorescence intensity.

In addition, as shown in Figs. S2D and S3D, the fluorescence intensity of solutions increased gradually if Mg2+ concentration increased from 0 to 40 mM, but decreased if more than 40 mM Mg2+ was used. This phenomenon was because Mg2+ can reduce the repulsive interaction between two DNA strands, but Mg2+ can also interact with the G-triplex structure, and hinders the interaction of ThT and cDNA-G31, therefore resulting in the decreased fluorescence intensity [27]. This experimental result was consistent with the previous report that the fluorescence signal of the G-triplex-ThT complex leveled off if high concentration of Mg2+ was used [25].

The effect of pH value of the solution on TNOS detection was also studied, as shown in Figs. S2E and S3E, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased with the pH values increasing from 5.3 to 7.3 and decreased if the pH value further increased. This was because the repulsive interaction between two negatively charged strands weakened as the pH values increased, which facilitated the hybridization of two DNA strands [28].

The reaction time between cDNA-G31 and TNOS was also studied, as shown in Figs. S2F and S3F, the F/F0 of solutions in the absence and presence of TNOS was increased from 10 min to 60 min and maintained at a constant value after 60 min. In conclusion, 200 nM cDNA-G31, 6 μM ThT, Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.3 contained 40 mM Na+ and 40 mM Mg2+) and the reaction time between cDNA-G31 and TNOS at 60 min were selected for TNOS detection.

The experimental conditions to detect Hg2+ were also optimized, as shown in Fig. S5, three DNA with different sequences (i.e., 6T-G31, 10T-G31, and 14T-G31) were selected to interact with Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity reached a maximum when five pairs of T was used in the presence of 300 nM Hg2+; in the absence of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of solutions constantly increased with the number of T increased. This phenomenon was because in the presence of Hg2+, more T bases generated more T-Hg2+-T pairs, more extended intramolecular double strands, and more tight linkages of bases to ThT, so the fluorescence intensity of the solution was enhanced. However, at low concentrations of Hg2+ (e.g., 300 nM), Hg2+ was quickly exhausted in the presence of T-rich DNA with a longer sequence, therefore resulting in the formation of fewer dsDNA, and declined fluorescence intensity; In the absence of Hg2+, the flexibility of ssDNA enhanced with the number of T increased, the steric hindrance during the G-triplex combined with ThT was decreased. Since more ThT molecules were able to be inserted into the longer DNA, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was constantly enhanced by increasing the number of T [29]. The difference of F/F0 was not significant by using 6T-G31 and 10T-G31, therefore, the relatively shorter DNA (i.e., 6T-G31) was applied to simplify the DNA sequence design procedure. As shown in Figs. S6-S9, 150 nM of 6T-G31, 6 μM of ThT, with a reaction time of 10 min, pH value of 7.5 was selected for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TNOS and Hg2+ in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal experimental conditions, TNOS and Hg2+ were detected. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased with the TNOS concentration increasing from 0 to 200 nM; the linear calibration curve for TNOS detection is
F
F 0
= 0.0233
C
T N O S
+ 0.8964

CTNOS refers to the concentrations of TNOS. The linear detection range is 2 nM–200 nM. The detection limit is 2.22 nM (3 times the standard deviation of the blank divided by the slope of the calibration curve, 3σ/s).

Hg2+ was also quantitatively measured using this method, as shown in Fig. 2C and D, the fluorescence signal of the solution increased with increasing the concentration of Hg2+ from 2 to 300 nM; the calibration curves for Hg2+ detection are
F
F 0
= 0.3556
log
C
H
g
2 +
+ 0.9868

(the concentration of Hg2+ is from 2 nM to 40 nM), and
F
F 0
= 1.8053
log
C
H
g
2 +
− 1.4873

(the concentration of Hg2+ is from 40 nM to 300 nM). CHg 2+ refers to the concentration of Hg2+. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.75 nM for Hg2+ (3 times the standard deviation of the blank divided by the slope of the calibration curve, 3σ/s).

As shown in Tables S2-S3, our methods' detection range or LOD is similar to previous reports. Although the LOD of this assay is in the nM range and is not as sensitive as some DNA detection methods using the signal amplification approach, our method has its advantages, like being easy-to-conduct, label-free, without multiple signal amplification processes, without complicated DNA design and sophisticated instrument. We believe that by using signal amplification protocol, as well as carefully designing the sequence of DNA probe, the sensitivity of our methods will be further enhanced, therefore facilitating food safety evaluation in the future.

Selectivity is essential for a successful analytical method. As shown in Fig. 3 , in the presence of TNOS, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased, but in the presence of three random sequence nucleotides (Oligo 1, Oligo 2, and Oligo 3), a relatively low level of fluorescence intensity was observed. Our method can also be used for discriminating DNA that has 2 or 5 mismatched bases. The fluorescence intensity of solutions decreased with mismatched bases increased from 2 to 5.

The selectivity of this method for Hg2+ detection was also studied. As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased significantly in the presence of Hg2+, but not changed a lot in the presence of many other metal ions (e.g., Ag+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, and Mn2+). This phenomenon originates from the specific interaction between Hg2+ and thymine [30]. Therefore, we demonstrate that our method has satisfactory selectivity towards TNOS and Hg2+.

Considering the probability of the actual samples containing TNOS and Hg2+ simultaneously, we can detect TNOS and Hg2+ separately, i.e., using cDNA-G31 to determine TNOS, and using 6T-G31 to determine Hg2+ in the samples. Our preliminary experimental results indicate that the 6T-G31 probe has no response to TNOS, and the cDNA-G31 also has no response to Hg2+ (data not shown here). Therefore, it is not a problem if one sample contains these two species (e.g., TNOS and Hg2+) simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The utility of this fluorescent method
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the utility of this method for practical applications, DNA was firstly extracted from the transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans using a commercial DNA extraction kit. The dilution experiment was used for the proof-of-concept purpose [7]. The transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans extractions were diluted 1, 2, and 4 times [10], amplified using PCR, and detected using our fluorescent method. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased with increasing the concentration of the extracting solutions from 25% to 100%, but not changed a lot even using the undiluted non-transgenic soybeans extractions. In addition, agarose gel electrophoresis was used for determining the presence of transgenic fragments. As shown in Fig. 4B, the band around 195 bp indicated the presence of transgenic components in the transgenic soybean samples; there was no such band for the non-transgenic soybean sample, which indicated our method can be used for discriminating the transgenic and non-transgenic samples.

The capacity of our method to detect Hg2+ in lake water was also studied. As shown in Table S4, different amounts of Hg2+ were added to the lake water (the concentration of Hg2+ in the original sample was lower than the detection limit of the inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry) and detected using our fluorescent method, the recoveries of Hg2+ ranged from 94.33% to 101.93% by using the standard addition method, which indicated that our approach had reasonable accuracy, and was promising for the determination of Hg2+ in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CD spectroscopy and PAGE experiment
ce:section.ce:para: CD spectroscopy and PAGE were utilized to further investigate the mechanism of this method; as shown in Fig. 4C, the positive peaks at 210 nm and 260 nm for TNOS and cDNA-G31 samples indicated that these two DNA strands had parallel G-triplex structure. A significant negative peak appeared at around 430 nm after the addition of ThT, which indicated that ThT could insert into the TNOS and cDNA-G31, and the interaction between ThT and cDNA-G31 was more potent than that of ThT with TNOS [22]. The peak intensity at 210 nm decreased since the parallel G-triplex structure was distorted, and the peak intensity at 260 nm increased since the DNA structure was changed from B-type to A-type [27]. After the hybridization of cDNA-G31 and TNOS, the intensity at 210 nm reached highest, accompanied by the appearance of a positive peak at 260 nm and a negative peak at 245 nm. This phenomenon indicated that the hybridization of cDNA and TNOS resulted in the duplex structure, which stabilized the G-triplex, and facilitated its combination with ThT molecules [31]. For the CD spectrum of the TNOS-cDNA-G31-ThT complex, the signal intensity at 260 nm reached the maximum, while the signal intensity at 210 nm decreased, which was consistent with the results that ThT was able to insert into cDNA-G31, and induced the transformation of DNA structure from B-type to A-type; the signal intensity at 260 nm and 430 nm increased, and the peak positions were red-shifted, which indicated the structure of TNOS-cDNA-G31-ThT complex was more stable than cDNA-G31-ThT complex [32] and confirmed our assumption that the formation of adjacent dsDNA increased the stability of G-triplex-ThT complex, and significantly enhanced its fluorescence intensity.

CD spectroscopy was also used to study the interaction between 6T-G31, Hg2+, and ThT. As shown in Fig. S11, the positive peak of 6T-G31 at 260 nm red-shifted after interacting with Hg2+. In comparison, the positive peak at 210 nm was not changed a lot, which indicated that the thymine bases interact with Hg2+ and formed more stable intramolecular T-Hg2+-T pairs [16]. After 6T-G31 interacted with ThT, the positive peak intensity at 260 nm enhanced significantly, accompanied by the appearance of a negative peak at 425 nm, which indicated that ThT was able to bind with 6T-G31; the signal intensity at 210 nm decreased dramatically, which indicated the G-triplex structure was changed or disrupted [31]. After the interaction of Hg2+ and ThT with 6T-G31, the CD spectrum does not change a lot compared with the 6T-G31-ThT complex. There were two reasons to explain this phenomenon, 1) the volume of Hg ion is smaller compared with DNA (e.g., TNOS), its effect on the conformation change of 6T-G31 is not as strong as DNA or ThT; 2) there are only three newly generated T-Hg2+-T pairs in the presence of Hg2+, the relative small changes of 6T-G31-Hg2+ complex also contribute to the small change of the CD spectra. Nevertheless, the red shift of the spectra was indeed observed (260 nm and 425 nm), which indicated the enhanced conformation stability of the mixture after binding with Hg2+. We assume that apparent CD changes would be observed for longer DNA (e.g., 10T-G31, 14T-G31) since the stability of dsDNA will affect the stability of G-triplex-ThT. The exact mechanism will be carefully studied in our recent experiments. These experimental results were consistent with the previous reports [22,31].

PAGE is a suitable method to study DNA structure change. As shown in Fig. 4D, the migration rate of cDNA-G31 (lane 2) was slower than TNOS (lane 3) since cDNA-G31 had a longer sequence than TNOS; after interacting with ThT, a trailing band was observed for cDNA-G31-ThT (lane 4) and cDNA-G31+TNOS + ThT (lane 7), which indicated that the G-triplex could bind with ThT, the complex with additional molecule resulted in its electrophoresis rate slow down. The duplex structure formed by the interaction of TNOS and cDNA-G31 (lane 6) was verified by the appearance of a new band that moved much slower than cDNA-G31 or TNOS; after the addition of ThT (lane 7), a smeared band was observed which was similar to the cDNA-G31-ThT complex. However, the electrophoresis rate was slower than the cDNA-G31-ThT species (as indicated by the red circles in Fig. 4D). These experimental results were consistent with the fluorescence and CD experiments, and further confirmed our assumption that the dsDNA adjacent to G-triplex enhanced the stability of the G-triplex-ThT complex and synergistically enhanced the fluorescence intensity of ThT.

PAGE was also used to study the interaction between Hg2+ and 6T-G31. As shown in Fig. S12, there was a smear stripe after adding ThT into the 6T-G31 solution. In contrast, no significant change was observed after Hg2+ interacted with 6T-G31. This phenomenon was induced by the relatively few intramolecular T-Hg2+-T pairs (only three pairs), so there was no distinct difference in the motility of 6T-G31 and 6T-G31-Hg2+. The motility of 6T-G31-ThT-Hg2+ was slower than the 6T-G31-ThT and 6T-G31-Hg2+ samples, which confirmed the interaction among 6T-G31, ThT, and Hg2+, as well as the conformation changes of 6T-G31 induced by ThT and Hg2+.

In summary, we have developed a versatile sensing platform based on the conformation changes of intra- or inter-molecule dsDNA adjacent to the G-triplex-ThT. Intermolecular dsDNA was formed through the hybridization between TNOS and cDNA, intramolecular dsDNA was formed by T-Hg2+-T pairs. Since adjacent dsDNA enhances the stability of the G-triplex-ThT complex, which results in the enhanced fluorescence signal. This simple, label-free method was used to discriminate the transgenic and non-transgenic soybeans and detect Hg2+ in lake water. The detection limits of TNOS and Hg2+ were 2.22 nM and 0.75 nM, and the detection ranges were 2–200 nM and 2–300 nM, respectively. Our approach is selective and sensitive, does not require sophisticated instruments, tedious operation procedures, complicated functional materials, and fluorescence molecular labeled DNA, which dramatically reduces detection costs. Although TNOS and Hg2+ are chosen here to establish this assay by proof-of-principle experiment, our approach is transferable to other analytical requirements, such as quantitatively determining antibiotics, mycotoxins, pesticides, and other heavy metal ions in environmental and agricultural samples, as well as the microRNA detection for clinical application by changing the DNA sequences.
Chengke Wang contributed to the experiments' design, data analysis, and manuscript preparation, Jiangyu Li performed the experiments, Ping Wang contributed to the manuscript preparation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305032), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M551522), and Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (1402073B).
